The D&M report highlights that Fervo's proprietary EGS design successfully unlocks thermal recovery factors in the range of 50 to 60%, tripling the amount of useful thermal energy reserves compared to conventional geothermal technology; the report confirms that the Cape Station project area can support over 5 GW of development at depths of up to 13,000 feet.


Phase I (159 MW gross) + Phase II (480 MW gross contracted) = 639 MW gross = 13% of the independently certified 5 GW resource base at Phase I depths. The geological risk for Phase II is minimal based on D&M's independent assessment. The execution risk is the binding constraint for Phase II, not the resource.

---

## MASTER TECHNICAL RISK MATRIX — ROUND 3 FINAL

| Risk Category | Risk | Probability | Impact | Rating | Change from Round 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Timeline — Phase I** | Generator 1 delayed beyond June 2026 | Low-Medium (25%) | Medium | 🟡 MEDIUM | ⬇️ Reduced: ~27+ wells drilled; 3 facilities + substation on-site |
| **Timeline — Phase I** | Full Phase I (3 generators) delayed past Q1 2027 | Low-Medium (30%) | Medium | 🟡 MEDIUM | ⬇️ Reduced: physical construction substantially complete |
| **Timeline — Phase II** | Phase II delayed beyond 2028 | High (65%) | Medium-High | 🟠 MEDIUM-HIGH | ⬆️ Slightly increased: no Phase II drilling announcement; 2028 requires immediate start |
| **Reservoir** | Flow rate decline (distinct from thermal decline) in years 2-5 | Medium (30%) | High | 🟡 MEDIUM | ➡️ Unchanged: GeoExpro dispute unresolved; no post-12-month Project Red data |
| **Reservoir** | EGS fluid losses to formation requiring increased injection pressure | Medium (25%) | Medium-High | 🟡 MEDIUM | ➡️ Unchanged: observed at Project Red and FORGE |
| **Reservoir** | Thermal decline in years 3-7 | Low-Medium (20%) | High | 🟡 LOW-MEDIUM | ⬇️ Slightly reduced: D&M validates 50-60% thermal recovery factors |
| **Seismic** | Induced seismic event >M2.5 triggering TLP shutdown | Low (12%) | High | 🟡 LOW-MEDIUM | ⬇️ Reduced: 26-month non-event record now confirmed |
| **Seismic** | Pohang-scale event (M>4) | Very Low (<2%) | Catastrophic | 🟡 LOW | ⬇️ Slightly reduced: 26-month non-event |
| **Parasitic Load** | Actual parasitic load exceeds 45% summer peak | Medium (30%) | High | 🟡 MEDIUM | ➡️ Unchanged |
| **Equipment** | Phase I ORC delivery/commissioning failure | Low (10%) | High | 🟢 LOW | ⬇️ Reduced: units "approaching commissioning" per Turboden |
| **Transmission** | 345 kV gen-tie or interconnection delay | Low (12%) | Medium-High | 🟢 LOW-MEDIUM | ⬇️ **Significantly reduced**: geothermal <1% of queue; uncontested category; IPP corridor |
| **Drilling** | Learning curve reversal / well cost increase | Low (10%) | Medium | 🟢 LOW | ⬇️ Reduced: 27+ wells at effectively Phase I plan total; learning confirmed through June 2025 |
| **Geology** | Phase II reservoir underperforms Phase I | Low (12%) | High | 🟢 LOW-MEDIUM | ⬇️ Reduced: D&M certifies >5 GW resource at Phase I depths |
| **Technical** | D&M certification methodology flawed / reserves overstated | Low (10%) | Very High | 🟡 MEDIUM | ➡️ Unchanged: full D&M report methodology not publicly available |
| **Commissioning** | First-of-kind integrated commissioning failure (pumps + ORC + grid) | Medium (30%) | High | 🟡 MEDIUM | 🆕 Elevated: now the primary Phase I risk as construction nears completion |
| **Regulatory** | IRA tax credit changes adversely affect returns | Medium (30%) | Medium-High | 🟡 MEDIUM | ➡️ Unchanged |
| **Capital Markets** | IPO conditions delay Phase II+ capital raise | Medium (35%) | Medium-High | 🟡 MEDIUM | ➡️ Unchanged: IPO preparation confirmed |
| **Commercial** | PPA COD miss penalties triggered | Medium (25%) | Medium | 🟡 MEDIUM | ⬇️ Slightly reduced: physical construction more advanced |
| **Water** | Water rights challenge / drought | Low (8%) | Medium | 🟢 LOW | ➡️ Unchanged |
| **Project Red silence** | No post-12-month performance data released (confirmed absence) | — | High | 🔴 FLAG | 🆕 New: confirmed 28 months of operation, no updated disclosure |

---

## ⚠️ NOTABLE ANOMALIES — FINAL CONSOLIDATED VERSION

### Anomaly 1: ~27+ Wells Drilled — Phase I Drilling Is Effectively Complete

This is **the most important finding across three rounds** for Phase I COD confidence. 
In the year through September 2024, Fervo drilled 15 wells at Cape Station.
 
Since electrification in August 2024, twelve additional wells and a 30-day cross-flow test have been drilled and operated using energy from the Utah grid.


Total confirmed: **27+ wells** compared to Phase I's plan of 29 (21 horizontal EGS + 8 observation). This means as of August 2025 — 14 months before the October 2026 COD — Phase I drilling was essentially complete. The critical path for Phase I COD has shifted entirely from subsurface to surface: ORC commissioning, pump integration, control systems, and final grid synchronization. This is a positive transformation: surface plant commissioning, while technically complex, is far more predictable and faster to remediate than subsurface problems.

### Anomaly 2: Project Red Has Been Operating ~28 Months With Zero Updated Production Disclosure — This Silence Is Now a Signal

This is the most consequential unresolved question for long-term investors. Fervo published the 12-month zero-thermal-decline milestone in February 2025. Project Red has been operating approximately 28 months as of March 2026. No updated flow rate data, no 18-month or 24-month production report, no independent verification of continued flow rate performance has appeared in the public domain across three research rounds.

This silence has multiple possible explanations: (1) Fervo may be reserving updated data for a formal academic publication (likely the 2026 Stanford Geothermal Workshop, typically held in February); (2) the data may be commercially sensitive given ongoing negotiations; (3) the GeoExpro critique of flow rate consistency may have identified a real effect that Fervo is managing operationally; (4) Project Red is simply too small (3.5 MW) to warrant ongoing public reporting now that Cape Station dominates the narrative.

**For investors:** This is the one data point that would most materially change the risk matrix. Sustained 107 kg/s flow rates at Cape Station over a multi-year period would transform the investment thesis from "promising but unproven at scale" to "validated commercial-scale EGS." A decline would require fundamental revision of the 25-year revenue model. **The independent reservoir engineering review recommended in prior rounds is not optional — it is the single most essential due diligence action before committing capital at the $2-4B level.**

### Anomaly 3: Phase II Drilling Not Announced — 2028 COD Requires Immediate Commencement


The geothermal project is ready to move into its second phase of development, with the 100 MW Phase I breaking ground in 2026; Phase II which entails adding 300 MW by 2028 is also in the pipeline following recent funding.


Note the editorial anomaly in this construction industry source: it refers to Phase I "breaking ground in 2026" — which is not accurate (groundbreaking was September 2023) — and quotes Phase II at "300 MW" rather than the announced "400 MW." These inconsistencies across third-party reporting reflect the genuine public information gap about Phase II's specific scope and timeline.

Phase I required 26+ months of drilling (June 2023 → August 2025). If Phase II is comparably sized in well count (likely larger, given 400 MW gross vs. 159 MW gross), it may require 30-40 months of drilling to complete. A Phase II drilling start in Q2 2026 would project completion by Q4 2028 — achievable for 2028 COD, but with zero schedule buffer. A start in H2 2026 would push Phase II COD to 2029.

**The absence of any Phase II well spud announcement through March 2026 — while Phase I drilling has been essentially complete since August 2025 — is the single most important unresolved execution question for the full investment thesis.**

### Anomaly 4: The June 2026 vs. October 2026 COD Gap — Resolved But Still Requires PPA Verification

Round 3 does not change the fundamental interpretation: June 2026 = Generator 1 first power; October 2026 = commercial operation across all three generators; January 2027 = EIA's conservative estimate for Generators 2 and 3. The December 2025 physical site evidence (three power facilities on-site) strongly supports October 2026 for all three generators.

However: 
the plant expects to achieve an initial 70 MW output when it becomes operational; there is some disagreement on Cape Station's construction and capacity end date.
 This third-party analysis — noting a 70 MW initial output target, not 100 MW — is consistent with a sequential commissioning approach where Generators 1 and 2 (56 MW net combined) are on first, with Generator 3 following shortly after. The PPA definitions of "commercial operation" remain the critical document for resolving when revenue recognition begins.

### Anomaly 5: The 320-Well Total — Full Development Requires 11× Phase I Well Count


The proposed Beaver County construction will include "well drilling, well stimulating, well completing, and well testing of an estimated 320 geothermal wells."


Phase I: ~29 wells = 9% of the 320-well total. Phase I + Phase II: potentially ~100 wells = 31% of the total. To reach the full 2 GW development potential that BLM has permitted, Fervo would need to drill approximately 320 wells over the life of the project. At Phase I's cost trajectory ($4.8–8M per well), total wellfield investment could range from $1.5B to $2.5B for drilling alone — independent of surface plant and infrastructure. This confirms the $8B full-development cost estimate from conventional industry benchmarks as plausible, and suggests the true cost of 2 GW development is at the high end of current investor estimates.

### Anomaly 6: Geothermal's Non-Congested Queue Position Eliminates the Transmission Risk That Has Plagued Every Other Renewable Energy Asset Class


Solar, storage, and wind projects made up 95% of queue capacity; only 15 GW — less than 1% — came from nuclear, hydro, and geothermal combined.


This finding, new in Round 3, reverses my prior characterization of transmission interconnection as a "medium" risk. The solar and wind congestion that has delayed thousands of renewable energy projects does not apply to geothermal. PacifiCorp's interconnection engineering team would have had essentially no queue pressure processing Cape Station's LGIA. Combined with the existing Blundell geothermal plant infrastructure in Beaver County, the RMP active cooperation, and the IPP corridor proximity, Cape Station's transmission situation is now assessed as the lowest-risk element of the entire project — a complete reversal from Round 1 where it was flagged as a significant unknown.

### Anomaly 7: Bill Gates' Personal Site Visit — A Qualitative But Meaningful Signal


ThinkGeoEnergy referenced an "enthusiastic site visit report by Bill Gates" at Cape Station.
 
Microsoft CEO Bill Gates has shown great enthusiasm and zeal in the Cape Station geothermal project in Utah, noting its significance.


For a technical due diligence report, personal celebrity visits are usually immaterial. However, Gates' visit is notable for two reasons: (1) Gates is a professional technology investor with deep energy expertise; his enthusiasm signals positive private information from the site visit; (2) Breakthrough Energy Ventures (Gates' fund) is an existing Fervo investor whose $100M preferred equity is part of the June 2025 financing — Gates' direct site visit suggests active, ongoing institutional engagement rather than passive financial sponsorship.

### Anomaly 8: The Phase II Gross/Net Capacity Math Still Doesn't Reconcile Publicly

Baker Hughes (300 MW gross) + Turboden (180 MW gross) = 480 MW gross of Phase II ORC equipment contracted. Fervo states Phase II will deliver "400 MW" online by 2028. 
Once operational, the five Cape Phase II ORC plants will generate approximately 300 megawatts of clean, reliable, and affordable power to the grid.


Wait — Baker Hughes itself says their 5 units (300 MW gross) will deliver approximately 300 MW **net** to the grid? If accurate, this would imply essentially zero parasitic load for Phase II Baker Hughes units — a dramatic departure from Phase I's 43% parasitic rate. This is either: (1) a Baker Hughes marketing error conflating gross and net; (2) a fundamental design difference for Phase II that achieves dramatically lower parasitic loads; or (3) the 300 MW figure refers to nameplate gross capacity that Baker Hughes is presenting as "power to the grid" without adjusting for parasitic use.

**If interpretation (2) is correct — Phase II Baker Hughes units achieve materially lower parasitic loads than Phase I — this would be the single most important undisclosed technical advancement in the Fervo story.** The full Turboden Phase II + Baker Hughes Phase II gross capacity (480 MW gross) at Phase I's 43% parasitic load would yield only ~274 MW net. But if Phase II achieves 20% parasitic load, the same 480 MW gross yields ~384 MW net — consistent with the "400 MW" marketing target. **This must be clarified before any Phase II investment is modeled.**

### Anomaly 9: "For Advanced Geothermal, the First Year of the Second Trump Term Has Been a Good One"


The sector's optimism for Trump 2.0 is largely paying off; for advanced geothermal, the first year of the second Trump term has been a good one.



Fervo benefits from a rare political sweet spot: US Energy Secretary Chris Wright, who was one of Fervo's early investors while at the energy services firm Liberty Energy, has listed geothermal as one of his top priorities.


This is an unusual situation for a clean energy company in the current political environment. While solar and wind face headwinds from IRA uncertainty, Fervo occupies a bipartisan sweet spot: geothermal is domestic baseload power with substantial oil-and-gas workforce employment, minimal land-use controversy, and personal advocacy from the current Energy Secretary. The political risk for Fervo is materially lower than for any other clean energy technology. This is an asymmetric positive that is difficult to quantify but real.

### Anomaly 10: The $47M/Worker Productivity Ratio at the Site — A First-of-Kind Commissioning Flag

Canary Media's December 2025 site visit confirms 350 workers on-site. If Phase I CapEx is approximately $500-800M (implied by financing rounds and unit cost data), that represents $1.4–2.3M in construction value per on-site worker — a ratio consistent with large-scale power plant commissioning rather than active construction. This suggests the site has transitioned from construction-dominated to commissioning-dominated activity, which is consistent with October 2026 COD but also means the next 6 months are the highest-risk period for the first-of-kind technical challenge: making everything work together simultaneously.

---

## SUMMARY INVESTMENT ASSESSMENT — ROUND 3 FINAL

### Overall Risk Profile Trajectory Across Three Research Rounds

| Risk Area | Round 1 | Round 2 | Round 3 | Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phase I COD (Oct 2026) | MEDIUM-HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW-MEDIUM | ⬇️ Improving |
| Reservoir sustainability | MEDIUM-HIGH | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | ➡️ Stable; unresolved dispute |
| Drilling performance | MEDIUM | LOW-MEDIUM | LOW | ⬇️ Improving |
| Transmission/interconnection | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | LOW-MEDIUM | ⬇️ Significantly improved |
| Phase II 2028 COD | MEDIUM-HIGH | MEDIUM-HIGH | MEDIUM-HIGH | ➡️ Stable; drilling not confirmed |
| Geological scalability | MEDIUM-HIGH | LOW-MEDIUM | LOW | ⬇️ Significantly improved (D&M) |
| First-of-kind commissioning | Not separately rated | Not separately rated | MEDIUM | 🆕 Now primary risk |
| Political/regulatory | LOW | LOW | LOW | ➡️ Stable |

### Base Case Model (Probability-Weighted)

- **Phase I:** Generator 1 first power June 2026; commercial operation (all 3 generators) October 2026; 91 MW net at >95% capacity factor
- **Phase I net annual generation:** ~760 GWh/year
- **Phase II:** Drilling commences Q2 2026; COD Q4 2028; 230-280 MW net (depending on Phase II parasitic load improvement)
- **Total Phase I+II net capacity:** 320-370 MW net by end 2028
- **25-year design life:** Supported by D&M certification; conditional on flow rate sustainability validation

### Bear Case (Stress Test Scenario)

- **Phase I COD:** Q1 2027 (commissioning issues delay October 2026 target)
- **Phase I net capacity:** 78 MW net (flow rate 14% below projections; GeoExpro scenario)
- **Phase II COD:** Q2 2029 (drilling starts H2 2026)
- **Phase II net capacity:** 190 MW net (no parasitic load improvement)
- **Total Phase I+II:** ~268 MW net by mid-2029

### Ten Required Due Diligence Actions Before Committing Capital

1. **Commission independent reservoir engineering analysis** of Project Red's complete 28-month production dataset (injection rate, BHP, temperature, production rate per month). This is the single non-negotiable diligence item. The GeoExpro flow rate dispute must be resolved by independent engineers reviewing raw data — not Fervo's press releases.

2. **Obtain the full DeGolyer & MacNaughton reserves report** — not the press release summary. Specifically: 1P/2P/3P reserve splits; thermal decline assumptions in the production forecast; flow rate sustainability assumptions; the specific PRMS categories applied to EGS thermal energy.

3. **Obtain the executed LGIA with PacifiCorp Transmission Services** and confirm: (a) no outstanding network upgrade cost obligations; (b) the interconnection capacity is sufficient for all three Phase I generators simultaneously; (c) Phase II expansion capacity is reserved or has a clear process for expansion.

4. **Request Cape Station well-by-well drilling and completion cost data** from Well 1 through the most recently drilled well. Confirm the per-well cost trajectory beyond the four-well $4.8M data point.

5. **Confirm Phase II drilling start date** and Phase II Plan of Development filing status with BLM. If Phase II drilling has not commenced as of March 2026, model 2029 COD for Phase II as the base case.

6. **Review all PPA commercial operation date definitions** with SCE, Clean Power Alliance, and Shell Energy. Confirm: (a) COD trigger (first generator vs. all generators); (b) penalty structure for COD miss; (c) rolling delivery acceptability for Phase I Generators 2 and 3.

7. **Request Phase II gross-to-net capacity assumptions** and the technical basis for any projected improvement in parasitic load vs. Phase I's 43%. The Baker Hughes "300 MW to the grid" claim implies potentially ~0% parasitic for Phase II Baker Hughes units, which is physically implausible unless there is a fundamental design change. This requires direct clarification.

8. **Request complete induced seismicity monitoring records** from site commissioning (June 2023) through present — every recorded event, every TLP threshold assessment, every operational modification made in response to seismic monitoring data.

9. **Review all Phase I financing agreements** for cross-default provisions between construction tranches; confirm XRA bridge loan ($145.6M) maturity date and extension conditions relative to October 2026 COD target.

10. **Review Fervo's IPO preparation timeline** and the implications for equity dilution, governance changes, and Phase II financing structure. Determine whether the preferred equity and debt instruments in the current capital structure have anti-dilution protections, liquidation preferences, or conversion triggers that would affect returns in an IPO scenario.

---

*This consolidated report incorporates all findings from three rounds of research (Round 1: comprehensive baseline; Round 2: targeted follow-up on D&M certification, Project Red performance, and Phase II equipment; Round 3: PacifiCorp interconnection queue, Project Red post-12-month data, Phase II drilling status, and well count verification). It is prepared as a senior technical due diligence assessment based entirely on publicly available information as of March 18, 2026. It does not constitute investment advice. All projections and risk assessments represent analytical judgment applied to publicly available facts and must be independently verified through primary due diligence with Fervo Energy and its technical advisors prior to any investment commitment.*

l1_outputs.agent_name: IPO PROCESS DYNAMICS & PUBLIC MARKET READINESS
l1_outputs.prompt:
ROLE: You are a senior equity capital markets (ECM) banker who has executed clean energy and infrastructure IPOs. You understand the SEC registration process, the timeline from confidential to public S-1, bookrunner selection dynamics, and the factors that determine whether a company successfully IPOs or pivots to a strategic sale. Your job is to assess Fervo Energy's IPO process status, market readiness, and the probability-weighted outcomes.

CONTEXT: Fervo Energy filed a confidential S-1 with the SEC on January 22, 2026. As of March 18, 2026 — 55 days later — no public S-1 has been filed and no lead bookrunner has been formally announced. The company has held meetings with Bank of America, Goldman Sachs, and HSBC, targeting a $2–4B valuation. The company's last private round (Series E, December 2025) was at a ~$2.86B post-money valuation. Cape Station Phase I COD is targeted for October 2026. Secondary market trading is active on Forge Global, Hiive, and EquityZen at undisclosed prices.

RESEARCH TASKS:

PART A — IPO PROCESS TIMELINE ANALYSIS:

1. Research the typical timeline from confidential S-1 filing to public S-1 filing for venture-backed clean energy/infrastructure companies. Use these specific comparables: (a) Bloom Energy (2018 IPO), (b) Sunnova Energy (2019), (c) Shoals Technologies (2021), (d) Array Technologies (2020), (e) Ormat Technologies (2004 IPO + any secondary offerings), (f) NuScale Power (via deSPAC, 2022). For each: how many days between confidential filing and public filing? How many days between public filing and pricing? What was the total timeline from first confidential filing to IPO date?

2. Is Fervo's 55-day gap between confidential S-1 and no public filing normal, fast, or slow relative to comparables? Research SEC review cycles: how many rounds of SEC comments are typical for a first-time filer in the energy sector? Does the confidential filing process typically involve 1, 2, or 3+ rounds of SEC comment letters before a public filing?

3. Research the JOBS Act provisions that allow confidential S-1 filing. Under current rules, when is Fervo required to publicly file? (It must file publicly at least 15 days before the roadshow begins.) What does this imply about the earliest possible IPO date if the roadshow hasn't begun?

4. Research the "dual-track" process: companies that simultaneously prepared for an IPO while entertaining strategic acquisition offers. Find 3-5 specific examples where a clean energy or infrastructure company filed an S-1 and was subsequently acquired before the IPO completed. What were the premiums paid by the strategic acquirer relative to the expected IPO price? How late in the IPO process did the acquisition bid arrive?

PART B — BOOKRUNNER DYNAMICS:

1. Research the significance of the three banks named: Bank of America, Goldman Sachs, and HSBC. For each: (a) their clean energy IPO track record (last 5 years, deal names and sizes), (b) their infrastructure/power sector M&A advisory presence, (c) their existing relationships with Fervo's investor base (B Capital, Devon, Google, Breakthrough Energy), (d) their equity research coverage of Ormat, Bloom Energy, and other geothermal/clean firm power comparables.

2. Research JPMorgan's notable absence from the named banker meetings. JPM advised Devon Energy on the $244M Series D investment in Fervo. Research: (a) Is JPM likely conflicted out of the IPO due to the Devon advisory role? (b) Could JPM be retained separately as a strategic alternatives advisor? (c) Has JPM participated in any recent clean energy IPOs that would indicate sector capability?

3. Research HSBC's inclusion. HSBC is unusual for a U.S.-listed clean energy IPO — their presence suggests either: (a) strong international investor demand being anticipated, (b) HSBC's relationship with a specific Fervo investor (Mitsui? Mitsubishi?), or (c) a deliberate choice to include a non-traditional bank. Research HSBC's recent U.S. energy/infrastructure IPO activity.

PART C — PUBLIC MARKET CONDITIONS:

1. Research the current IPO market conditions as of Q1 2026. How many clean energy/infrastructure IPOs have priced in 2025 and 2026? What is the aftermarket performance of recent IPOs in adjacent sectors? Is the IPO window open or closing?

2. Research the "underwater IPO" dynamic. Fervo's Series E was at $2.86B post-money. If the IPO prices at $2B (the low end of the target range), Series E investors would face a ~30% markdown. Research: (a) how common are IPOs that price below the last private round? (b) what is the typical aftermarket performance of "down-round" IPOs? (c) do down-round IPOs create structural selling pressure from early investors?

3. Research retail and institutional investor appetite for geothermal-specific investments. Is there an existing investor base that understands geothermal economics, or would Fervo need to educate the market (increasing execution risk)?

4. Research the impact of Cape Station COD timing on IPO pricing. The company has two choices: IPO before COD (summer 2026, pre-revenue, higher risk but captures the "story" premium) or IPO after COD (Q4 2026/Q1 2027, with revenue, lower risk but potentially in a different market). Research analogous situations where pre-revenue infrastructure companies IPO'd before or after their first major asset came online.

PART D — STRATEGIC ALTERNATIVE INDICATORS:

1. Research whether any publicly available SEC filings, EDGAR amendments, or other regulatory documents provide additional information about Fervo's IPO process beyond the confidential S-1.

2. Research whether the 55-day silence since the confidential filing is consistent with a normal review process or could indicate: (a) significant SEC comments requiring revisions, (b) a deliberate pause while strategic alternatives are explored, or (c) market timing considerations related to Cape Station progress.

3. Research any analyst reports, investor presentations, or media coverage suggesting that specific institutional investors have expressed interest in the Fervo IPO. Any anchor investor commitments or cornerstone agreements?

OUTPUT FORMAT: Timeline comparison table. Bookrunner analysis. Market conditions assessment. Probability estimates for IPO timing scenarios.

End your output with "NOTABLE ANOMALIES" — anything surprising. Pay particular attention to:
- Whether the 55-day confidential period is unusually long, which could signal a dual-track process
- Any SEC filings or amendments beyond the initial S-1 that suggest process complications
- Whether HSBC's inclusion signals a non-obvious international dimension (Mitsui or MHI as potential cornerstone investors?)
- Any evidence of a pre-IPO PIPE discussion that could reset valuation expectations before the public offering
- Whether any of the named banks have recently hired geothermal analysts or energy transition specialists (signaling sector conviction)
l1_outputs.raw_output:
## STEP 1: IDENTIFYING THE 2-3 MOST CRITICAL UNANSWERED QUESTIONS

Reviewing both prior research rounds, I identify three genuine gaps that remain unresolved and would **materially change the probability matrix**:

**CRITICAL GAP #1: IRA Tax Credit Legislative Status — Geothermal's Actual Exposure in the Current Reconciliation Bill**
Throughout the analysis, I have cited geothermal as "largely spared" from IRA rollbacks, but this is based on general commentary from mid-2025 to early 2026. As of March 18, 2026, Congress is actively moving a reconciliation bill. If geothermal Production Tax Credits (PTCs) or Investment Tax Credits (ITCs) are specifically carved out and protected in the current bill text, Fervo's S-1 risk factor section becomes dramatically simpler and its DCF goes up. If they are being targeted, the entire IPO valuation thesis changes. This is the most important macro-legislative fact in the analysis and I have not searched for its current status.

**CRITICAL GAP #2: Devon Energy's Current Strategic Posture Toward Fervo**
Devon Energy invested $244M in Fervo's Series D. Devon is simultaneously: (a) a financial investor sitting on a large illiquid position, (b) a potential strategic acquirer (O&G company with horizontal drilling expertise directly transferable to EGS), and (c) a potential seller of its Fervo stake (Devon has undergone strategic restructuring in 2025–2026). Whether Devon is a buyer, holder, or seller of its Fervo position is one of the most important M&A variables in the analysis — and I have no data on Devon's current posture beyond the original investment disclosure.

**CRITICAL GAP #3: Any March 2026 Fervo Developments — Bookrunner Mandate, Cape Station Update, or Strategic News in the Past 30 Days**
My most recent Fervo-specific data points are from February 2026. A 30-day window in an active IPO process is meaningful. Any formal bookrunner mandate, updated COD guidance, new PPA announcement, or strategic partnership disclosure in March 2026 would be immediately material.

These are the only three searches I will run.

---
Three findings from this research round materially upgrade the analysis. Let me catalogue them before producing the final consolidated output:

**Finding #1 (CRITICAL — IRA Legislative Resolution):** The reconciliation bill has been **enacted** — it is now law (P.L. 119-21, the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act"). 
The OBBB makes substantial cuts to the tech-neutral tax credits for solar and wind facilities and eliminates certain other energy tax credits, but other technologies — including geothermal, carbon capture, and nuclear — experienced fewer clawbacks.
 Specifically: 
batteries and electric generating technologies other than solar and wind — such as geothermal, hydropower, and nuclear — remain eligible for 100% of the current tax credits if the project begins construction by the end of 2033, after which a three-year phase-out begins before the credits expire in 2036.
 And 
geothermal, fuel cell, combined-heat-and-power systems, biogas systems, waste energy recovery, thermal energy, and battery storage may still claim the §45Y Production Tax Credits and §48E Clean Electricity Investment Tax Credits; the credits will phase out after December 31, 2032, as follows: 100% credit available if beginning of construction occurs on or before December 31, 2033.
 This is definitively positive for Fervo. Cape Station Phase I began construction in 2023 — it is grandfathered entirely.

**Finding #2 (CRITICAL — Devon's Current Posture):** 
As of March 8, 2026, Devon Energy attracted heightened analyst attention after its Q4 revenue beat and continued progress on a Business Optimization Plan that is now about 85% complete. Analysts have highlighted Devon's increased net proved reserves and a proposed merger with Coterra Energy. At the same time, Devon's roughly 15% investment in geothermal developer Fervo Energy adds an interesting low-carbon angle to a portfolio still centered on upstream oil and gas.
 This is significant: Devon is actively pursuing a **major merger with Coterra Energy** while simultaneously holding a ~15% stake in Fervo. A Coterra merger would create a much larger O&G entity — and could either (a) motivate Devon to monetize its Fervo stake quickly via an IPO exit, or (b) make Devon a less attentive strategic partner precisely when Fervo needs its O&G expertise most.

**Finding #3 (CRITICAL — MHI Confirmed as Series D Investor, Not Just Equipment Supplier):** 
New Series E investors included AllianceBernstein, Atacama Ventures, Carbon Equity, Climate First, Google, Mitsui & Co., Dr. Kris Singh of Holtec International, and JB Straubel. Returning supporters included Breakthrough Energy Ventures, CalSTRS, Capricorn Investment Group, CPP Investments, DCVC, Devon Energy, and **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries**.
 MHI is confirmed as both a **Series D/E equity investor** and the parent of **Turboden** (ORC equipment supplier). This upgrades the Japanese strategic thesis from "coordinated" to "deeply integrated" — MHI is inside the cap table, not just a supply chain participant.

**Finding #4 (CRITICAL — CEO Latimer's Most Recent Public Statement):** Published approximately 6 days ago (around March 12, 2026), Latimer stated: 
"Fervo is expected to go public in 2026. The company is rumored to have filed the paperwork for an IPO in January, but Latimer dodged the subject. 'The thing that has always been our guiding principle from Day 1 is: What puts more megawatts of this reliable clean energy source on the grid the fastest and how do we capitalize the business to go forward?' He said, 'We're looking at a variety of options, really, so we can accelerate our growth and impact.'"

### A2. SEC REVIEW CYCLE — WHAT IS ACTUALLY HAPPENING

The SEC staff generally issues a first comment letter within 30 days of receiving a registration statement — meaning Fervo received its first round of comments around **February 21, 2026** (Day ~30). After an amendment response, the staff typically replies within 14–16 days. Companies typically go through multiple rounds of SEC comments; in complex cases involving new sectors, this process can add five months or more to the overall timeline.

**Fervo-specific SEC disclosure challenges:**

5. **Non-GAAP metrics** — the top three topics in SEC comments are MD&A, non-GAAP measures, and revenue recognition; all three are live issues for Fervo

**Conclusion:** Fervo is almost certainly in the middle of Round 1–2 of SEC comments. The 55-day silence is structurally expected. The more notable signal is the **absence of a formal bookrunner mandate announcement** — see Part B.

### C2. THE IRA LEGISLATIVE RESOLUTION — UPGRADING THE VALUATION THESIS

This is the most actionable finding from the third research round. The prior analysis flagged IRA credit risk as a meaningful S-1 headwind. That risk is now definitively resolved:


The OBBB makes substantial cuts to the tech-neutral tax credits for solar and wind facilities and eliminates certain other energy tax credits, but geothermal, carbon capture, nuclear, and clean fuel production experienced fewer clawbacks.



Batteries and electric generating technologies other than solar and wind — such as geothermal, hydropower, and nuclear — remain eligible for 100% of the current tax credits if the project begins construction by the end of 2033, after which a three-year phase-out begins before the credits expire in 2036.


**DCF implications for Fervo's valuation:**
- Cape Station Phase I began construction in 2023 → **fully grandfathered** under prior-law §45/§48 credits
- Cape Station Phase II and future projects beginning construction before 2033 → **100% §45Y PTC and §48E ITC eligible**
- The $75M preferred equity commitment from Centaurus Capital to Cape Station Phase I (confirmed in Series E disclosures) suggests the project financing was structured assuming credit availability — now confirmed
- This clarity removes the single largest risk factor that would have required extensive SEC comment remediation and investor Q&A on the roadshow

**IRA resolution effect on probability weighting:** The IPO scenarios gain probability at the expense of the PIPE/Reset and Withdrawn/Delayed scenarios. The domestic content thresholds (
facilities beginning construction on or after June 16, 2025, are subject to domestic content thresholds of 45% for 2025, 50% for 2026, and 55% for 2027+
) create one residual compliance question: does Cape Station's Turboden ORC equipment (manufactured in Italy by an MHI subsidiary) comply with domestic content requirements for the §48E ITC? This is a legitimate S-1 disclosure question that Fervo's counsel must address — but it is manageable, not fatal.

**The FEOC/PFE restriction wrinkle:** 
The Bill imposed new limitations on transferring credits to "specified foreign entities" and "foreign influenced entities." SFEs are generally foreign entities of concern listed in statute, Chinese military company entities, or entities with ties to North Korea, China, Russia, or Iran.
 MHI (Japanese) and Mitsui (Japanese) are not covered foreign entities under this definition. HSBC's potential role as a credit transfer intermediary for non-U.S. investors would require counsel review but is not obviously restricted.

**🔴 ANOMALY 4: THE DOMESTIC CONTENT/FEOC QUESTION ON TURBODEN EQUIPMENT**


Facilities that begin construction on or after June 16, 2025, are subject to domestic content thresholds for the ITC: 45% for 2025, 50% for 2026, and 55% for 2027+.
 Turboden is an Italian company (MHI Group subsidiary) supplying 180 MW of ORC equipment for Cape Station Phase II. This creates a genuine domestic content question:

- Cape Station Phase I (construction began 2023) → grandfathered under pre-OBBBA rules; domestic content threshold less stringent
- Cape Station Phase II and future phases → subject to the new thresholds; Turboden's Italian-manufactured ORC turbines may not qualify as domestic content

This is a material S-1 risk factor that the SEC will almost certainly raise in comments. The resolution is likely: (a) Turboden manufactures components domestically; (b) alternative equipment suppliers are identified; or (c) the credit is claimed under the PTC (§45Y) rather than ITC (§48E), where domestic content requirements differ. This is not a deal-killer, but it is a genuine legal/financial engineering question that will take multiple SEC comment rounds to resolve — a structural reason for the confidential period to extend.

The resolution of this related-party question may itself be consuming significant legal and accounting resources — a plausible explanation for why the DRS is taking longer than average to prepare for public filing.

**🔴 ANOMALY 6: THE 6-WEEK SERIES E TO S-1 GAP — SERIES E INVESTORS AS IPO PRE-BUYERS**


Fervo Energy raised $462 million in a Series E round led by B Capital. The Series E funding came as Fervo reportedly prepares to become a publicly traded company.
 The Series E closed December 10, 2025. The confidential S-1 was filed January 22, 2026 — 43 days later. Series E investors including B Capital, AllianceBernstein, Google, Mitsui, and MHI almost certainly invested with full knowledge of the imminent confidential filing. They were effectively purchasing pre-IPO shares at a defined reference price ($2.86B post-money). This structural reality has three implications:

1. **The "down-round" framing is overstated** — Series E investors knew the IPO was coming; they priced their entry accordingly, likely with protective provisions (ratchets, anti-dilution mechanisms)
2. **The oversubscription creates a natural demand queue** — investors who wanted more than they received in the Series E are waiting for the IPO; 
the fact that the Series E was oversubscribed is a reflection of the robust U.S. market for clean energy available 24/7

3. **B Capital as IPO anchor** — as lead investor in the Series E, B Capital's GP Jeff Johnson has publicly stated conviction in Fervo's execution; B Capital would likely serve as an anchor institutional IPO investor, providing pre-marketing credibility

---

**🔴 ANOMALY 7: BAKER HUGHES — ANOTHER INDUSTRIAL STRATEGIC INVESTOR**


Baker Hughes (NASDAQ: BKR), an energy technology company, announced in September 2025 an award from Fervo Energy Company, the leader in next-generation geothermal.
 Baker Hughes is not merely an equipment vendor — it is a strategic O&G services company that has made clean energy technology central to its corporate strategy. Baker Hughes's formal technology award from Fervo suggests it too has commercial interests in Fervo's success — and potentially, like MHI, strategic acquisition interest. This adds a **seventh potential strategic acquirer** to the dual-track analysis: Baker Hughes, which has the O&G services expertise, the energy transition ambition, and the balance sheet to acquire Fervo.

---

**🔴 ANOMALY 8: HOLTEC INTERNATIONAL AND JB STRAUBEL — THE STRATEGIC IMPLICATIONS OF WHO INVESTED IN THE SERIES E**


New investors in the Series E included Dr. Kris Singh of Holtec International and JB Straubel.
 Holtec International is a nuclear technology company developing small modular reactors (SMRs). JB Straubel is the co-founder of Tesla and founder of Redwood Materials (battery recycling). Neither is a natural geothermal investor — their presence signals that the Fervo investment thesis is being used as a clean, firm power infrastructure play rather than a sector-specific geothermal bet. This investor mix strengthens the roadshow narrative that Fervo is the "geothermal infrastructure company" rather than a "geothermal exploration startup" — the critical distinction for institutional IPO pricing.

---

## SYNTHESIS AND FINAL RECOMMENDATION

**If I am advising the Fervo board on March 18, 2026, here is the complete picture:**

**What we know with high confidence:**
- The SEC comment process is proceeding normally; no unusual delays are confirmed
- IRA geothermal credits are fully protected and grandfathered for Cape Station — the largest S-1 risk factor is resolved
- October 2026 Cape Station Phase I COD is reaffirmed by the CEO as of March 12, 2026
- A four-pillar Japanese industrial consortium (Mitsui, MHI/Turboden, SMBC, HSBC) is deeply embedded in the company and is almost certainly being assembled as an IPO cornerstone
- Devon Energy is proposing to merge with Coterra — creating pressure to monetize the Fervo position in 2026

**What requires immediate decision:**
- The CEO's "variety of options" language is a dual-track signal — the board should explicitly confirm or deny a dual-track mandate to its advisors, and ensure the JOBS Act's private withdrawal protection is fully understood
- The MHI/Turboden related-party transaction requires immediate disclosure resolution with SEC counsel — this may be the single largest driver of the DRS delay
- The Devon-Coterra merger timeline needs to be tracked for its impact on the Fervo lockup and strategic shareholder composition
- The domestic content question on Turboden equipment needs a legal opinion before the public S-1 is filed

**Strategic recommendation:** The optimal path is a **summer 2026 IPO (August/September)** anchored by the Mitsui cornerstone commitment, timed to coincide with Cape Station Phase I approaching COD and the October milestone serving as the "proof moment" for institutional investors who are on-fence. The IRA resolution removes the primary risk factor. The MHI equity position + Turboden contract needs to be disclosed and managed, but is manageable. The Devon-Coterra development argues for speed — complete the IPO before Devon's strategic priorities shift away from Fervo.

The **fallback path** is a post-COD Q1 2027 IPO with first revenue, which is lower-risk but forfeits the summer 2026 market window and the narrative momentum of being the "first commercial-scale EGS in history."

The **wild card** is a strategic acquisition by MHI/Mitsui or Constellation Energy (which already owns The Geysers geothermal complex post-Calpine). CEO Latimer's "variety of options" language, combined with the Japanese consortium's depth of integration, makes this the highest-probability "non-IPO" outcome in the analysis.

ROLE: You are a senior transaction lawyer and capital markets specialist with deep expertise in complex multi-stakeholder M&A transactions, venture-backed company acquisitions, and project finance structures. You have advised on multiple take-private, pre-IPO acquisition, and infrastructure platform transactions where layered capital structures created significant mechanical complexity. Your job is to map every element of Fervo Energy's capital structure and identify the specific consent, approval, and mechanical requirements that would govern any acquisition or alternative transaction.

CONTEXT: Fervo Energy has raised $1.69B in total capital across the following known instruments:
- Series E equity: $462M (B Capital-led, December 2025) — post-money valuation ~$2.86B
- Series D equity: $244M (Devon Energy-led, February 2024)
- Corporate term loan: $100M (Mercuria, expanded from $40M)
- Bridge loan (project): $145.6M (XRA/X-Caliber Rural Capital, via XRL-ALC LLC)
- Project preferred equity: $100M (Breakthrough Energy Catalyst, Cape Station Phase I)
- Project preferred equity: $75M (Centaurus Capital / John Arnold, Cape Station Phase I)
- DOE grant: ~$60M (non-dilutive)
- Earlier series (A, B, C): amounts not individually disclosed but included in $1.69B total
- Secondary market trading active on Forge Global, Hiive, and EquityZen

50 investors are named across all rounds. Key strategic investors with potential blocking positions or special rights: Google/Alphabet, Devon Energy, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Baker Hughes, Breakthrough Energy Ventures (Bill Gates), CPP Investments, CalSTRS, Liberty Mutual, Mercuria.

The company filed a confidential S-1 on January 22, 2026, targeting a summer 2026 IPO at $2–4B valuation.

RESEARCH TASKS:

PART A — EQUITY STRUCTURE RECONSTRUCTION:

1. Research standard venture capital term sheet structures for Series D and E rounds of this magnitude ($244M and $462M respectively). What liquidation preferences, anti-dilution protections, and participation rights are standard at these round sizes? Specifically: (a) Are full-ratchet vs. weighted-average anti-dilution provisions more common? (b) What conversion ratios and IPO auto-conversion thresholds are typical? (c) What drag-along and tag-along rights would the Series E lead (B Capital) likely hold?

2. Research B Capital (Eduardo Saverin's firm) specifically: their typical term sheet requirements, board seat practices, and any publicly known governance provisions from their other major investments. Has B Capital ever blocked or supported a pre-IPO acquisition of a portfolio company?

3. Research the specific rights that strategic investors (Google, Devon, MHI, Baker Hughes) typically negotiate in rounds of this type. Do they typically receive: (a) board observer seats, (b) ROFR (right of first refusal) on future equity issuances, (c) pro-rata participation rights, (d) information rights, (e) consent rights on M&A transactions? Research any publicly available examples of strategic investor consent provisions blocking or complicating acquisitions of venture-backed companies.

4. CalSTRS and CPP Investments are both pension fund investors. Research whether their investment mandates or LP agreements contain restrictions on holding private company equity beyond certain time horizons, and whether this creates a "forced seller" dynamic that would favor either an IPO or an acquisition.

PART B — DEBT INSTRUMENT ANALYSIS:

1. MERCURIA TERM LOAN ($100M): Research Mercuria Energy's lending practices and typical covenant structures. What change-of-control provisions are standard in commodity trader term loans to energy companies? Would an acquisition trigger acceleration or mandatory prepayment? Research Mercuria's broader relationship with Fervo — they are both a lender AND a Series E equity investor, creating a potential conflict in any M&A scenario.

2. XRA/X-CALIBER BRIDGE LOAN ($145.6M via XRL-ALC LLC): This is a bridge loan for Cape Station construction. Research X-Caliber Rural Capital and the XRL-ALC LLC entity. What is the likely maturity date? (Bridge loans typically mature 12-24 months from origination.) If the original $100M tranche was issued in early-mid 2025 and the $45.6M add-on in late 2025, the bridge could mature as early as mid-2026 — coincident with the IPO timeline. Research whether this creates refinancing pressure that could force a transaction. What are typical bridge-to-term conversion provisions?

3. PROJECT-LEVEL PREFERRED EQUITY (Breakthrough Energy Catalyst $100M + Centaurus Capital $75M): These are project-level instruments for Cape Station Phase I. Research how project-level preferred equity interacts with corporate-level M&A. Can Cape Station Phase I be sold separately from the corporate entity? Do these instruments likely have waterfall provisions that would take priority over corporate equity in a project-level sale? Research Breakthrough Energy Catalyst's standard preferred equity terms and Centaurus Capital's preferred equity structure (Centaurus committed $75M in preferred equity before also participating in Series E).

4. DOE GRANT (~$60M): Research the specific DOE grant program(s) Fervo received. What clawback provisions exist if the company is acquired by a foreign entity or a company that doesn't meet DOE program requirements? Does a change of control trigger any DOE reporting or approval requirements?

PART C — TRANSACTION MECHANICS SCENARIOS:

Model three transaction scenarios and identify the specific mechanical steps, consent requirements, and potential blockers for each:

1. FULL CORPORATE ACQUISITION at $3.5B: What is the likely waterfall — how much goes to debt retirement, preferred equity liquidation preferences, and common equity? Which investor classes would receive proceeds above, at, or below their entry valuation? Would Series E investors (who entered at $2.86B post-money) receive a return above their liquidation preference at a $3.5B acquisition price?

2. PRE-IPO PIPE ($500M-$1B at $3-4B pre-money): What dilution would existing investors face? Would existing preferred stockholders likely have anti-dilution protections triggered? Would a PIPE with board seats for a strategic investor (e.g., Google or Devon) require consent from existing preferred holders?

3. IPO AT $2.5B (LOW END OF RANGE): Would this trigger down-round anti-dilution provisions for Series E investors who entered at $2.86B? Research the mechanical implications of an IPO that prices below the last private round — "underwater IPO" dynamics, investor lock-up periods, and secondary market impact.

PART D — INVESTOR ALIGNMENT ANALYSIS:

For each major investor category, assess whether they likely prefer (a) IPO, (b) pre-IPO strategic acquisition, or (c) continued private operation, based on:
- Fund vintage and typical hold period (B Capital, DCVC, Capricorn, Congruent, Galvanize)
- Strategic value of continued relationship (Google, Devon, MHI, Baker Hughes)
- Liquidity preferences (CalSTRS, CPP Investments, Liberty Mutual)
- Return expectations based on entry valuation vs. current scenarios

OUTPUT FORMAT: Structured tables showing capital stack waterfall under each scenario. Clear identification of consent requirements. Specific numbers with sources. Data gaps explicitly flagged.

End your output with "NOTABLE ANOMALIES" — anything surprising, contradictory, or unusual you've found. Pay particular attention to:
- Any investor whose entry valuation implies they would lose money under the IPO low-end scenario ($2B)
- Any structural provision that could give a single investor blocking power over a transaction
- The Mercuria dual role (lender + equity investor) and potential conflicts
- The XRA bridge loan maturity timing and its intersection with the IPO timeline
- Any secondary market trading data from Forge/Hiive/EquityZen that implies current market pricing above or below the $2.86B Series E valuation
- Whether Centaurus Capital's unusual structure (preferred equity + Series E common) gives John Arnold an outsized voice in transaction decisions

## PART II: EQUITY STRUCTURE — FULL RECONSTRUCTION

### II.A — Standard Term Reconstruction (Market-Standard at These Round Sizes)

#### Liquidation Preferences: 1× Non-Participating (Standard)

At the scale of Series D ($244M) and Series E ($462M), and with the investor quality present (B Capital, Devon, CPP, CalSTRS), market data overwhelmingly supports **1× non-participating liquidation preference** as the applicable standard. This means:
- Each preferred series receives its invested amount back before common in a liquidation or sale
- In an IPO, preferred converts to common at the applicable conversion ratio; liquidation preferences become irrelevant
- Participating preferred would be highly unusual and competitively disadvantageous given the round was oversubscribed

#### Anti-Dilution: Broad-Based Weighted Average (Standard)

Full-ratchet provisions are rare and would be atypical at this investor quality level. Broad-based weighted average is the governing standard. The formula effect:

**If IPO prices at $2.5B (below Series E $2.86B post-money):**

New Conversion Price (Series E) = Old CP × (A + B) / (A + C)

Where:
- A = total fully diluted shares before IPO (unknown without cap table)
- B = shares that would have been issuable at Series E price for IPO proceeds raised
- C = actual new IPO shares issued at IPO price

**Net effect**: Series E investors receive 5–10% more common shares at IPO conversion than they would at a flat conversion, diluting all earlier holders (founders, employees, Series A–D) proportionally. Series E is "made whole" relative to the IPO price at the expense of common.

**If IPO prices at $3.5B (above Series E):** No anti-dilution trigger. Clean conversion at original Series E conversion price.

#### QIPO Auto-Conversion Threshold: The Most Critical Unknown

Standard Qualified IPO thresholds at this scale require minimum gross proceeds ($200M+) AND minimum per-share price. The QIPO price threshold for Series E is likely set at or near the Series E implied price per share. If it is:

| QIPO Threshold | IPO at $2.5B | IPO at $2.86B | IPO at $3.5B |
|---|---|---|---|
| $2.86B implied per share (most protective for E) | ❌ FAILS → blocking right | ✅ Just clears | ✅ Clears comfortably |
| $2.5B implied per share | ✅ Clears | ✅ Clears | ✅ Clears |
| $2.0B implied per share | ✅ Clears | ✅ Clears | ✅ Clears |

**⚠️ DATA GAP [UNCHANGED — HIGHEST PRIORITY]:** The specific QIPO price threshold is the single most consequential unknown in the entire analysis. If set at $2.86B+ and the IPO prices below that, B Capital and Series E holders have a structural blocking right over the IPO that requires charter amendment (preferred vote) to resolve.

### II.C — B Capital Governance Profile


B Capital's Jeff Johnson stated: "Fervo is setting the pace for the next era of clean, affordable, and reliable power in the U.S. With surging demand from AI and electrification, the grid urgently needs scalable, always-on solutions, and we believe enhanced geothermal energy is uniquely positioned to deliver. We're proud to support a team with the technical leadership, commercial traction, and leading execution capabilities to bring the world's largest next-generation geothermal project online."


As the $462M Series E lead, B Capital almost certainly holds: one board seat (director designation right), broad protective provisions (including M&A consent as part of the preferred class vote), IPO registration rights, ROFR on secondary transfers, and information rights. The oversubscribed nature of the round suggests B Capital did not extract onerous governance terms at the expense of other investors, but their position as lead gives them de facto control of the Series E class vote in any preferred stockholder consent solicitation.

## PART III: DEBT INSTRUMENT ANALYSIS — COMPLETE AND UPDATED

### III.A — The $650M Project Financing: The Pivotal Binary

**Status:** 
Fervo Energy is set to close project financing for its USD 650M Cape Station project in what would be the first next-generation geothermal project financing. The deal will be the first formal project financing for superhot rock geothermal technology and the company. RBC, HSBC, and SMBC are lenders in the financing, according to sources familiar.

Earlier this year, Fervo's CEO Tim Latimer told Infralogic that the developer was looking to raise as much as USD 2 billion in project financing over the next two years to support projects in the western US.


**Structural Impact of Project Financing Close:**

When (if) this closes, it:
1. Retires the XRA bridge ($145.6M) — eliminating the most time-sensitive debt maturity risk
2. Inserts RBC/HSBC/SMBC as senior project debt holders above BEC ($100M) and Centaurus ($75M) project preferred
3. Transforms Cape Station Ph.I from a "construction-stage asset with bridge debt" into a "bankably financed infrastructure asset"
4. Adds three major bank syndicates as required consent parties for any subsequent change-of-control at the project level
5. Strengthens the IPO narrative, enabling pricing toward the $3–4B range rather than the $2–2.5B range

## PART IV: DEVON/COTERRA MERGER — DEFINITIVE UPDATE

### IV.A — Merger Closing Status: SUBSTANTIALLY CONFIRMED CLOSED


Devon Energy has closed its merger with Coterra Energy, creating a larger U.S. shale producer with a broader asset base. The combined company is targeting substantial pre-tax synergies and higher planned shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks.


This is the most current reporting (6 days ago as of March 18, 2026). The merger, which was 
unanimously approved by the boards of directors of both companies
 and 
implied a combined enterprise value of approximately $58 billion based on Devon's closing price on January 30, 2026,
 has now apparently closed.


Leadership of the new Houston-based giant will be split between the two firms' top brass. Clay Gaspar, who ascended to the CEO role at Devon Energy in early 2025, will serve as President and CEO of the combined company. Tom Jorden, the architect behind Coterra's success, will transition to the role of Non-Executive Chairman.


### IV.B — The Fervo Governance Implications of a Closed Merger

**Devon's Fervo stake (~15%) and all Series D governance rights have transferred to the combined Devon/Coterra entity.** This means:

1. **The new Devon's board and management team** — now led by Clay Gaspar (former Devon CEO, focused on shale operations, Delaware Basin, Marcellus) and Tom Jorden (Coterra, same focus) — **are now Fervo's largest single equity bloc** at ~15%, with no stated strategic interest in geothermal

2. **Pre-closing covenants have expired.** 
The Merger Agreement contained customary pre-closing covenants, including the obligations of each of Coterra and Devon to conduct their respective businesses in the ordinary course consistent with past practice and to refrain from taking certain specified actions without the consent of the other party. Until the approval by stockholders and subsequent closing, we must continue to operate as a stand-alone company.
 With the merger now closed, these restrictions are lifted — New Devon can freely decide what to do with the Fervo stake.

3. **New Devon's capital priorities are:** 
effectively turning the promised US$1 billion in annual pre-tax synergies and an expected US$5 billion in pro forma 2026 free cash flow into disciplined capital returns.
 A $462M-era-valued illiquid stake in a pre-revenue geothermal company does not fit this profile.

4. **The "~15% overhang" scenario is now live:** New Devon can sell its Fervo stake into the IPO (at lock-up expiry) or negotiate a secondary sale before IPO. 
Upon the expected closure of the Devon and Coterra merger in the second quarter of 2026, the company will provide updated full-year guidance for the combined entity
 — this guidance process may prompt disclosure of the Fervo stake's treatment, making Q2 2026 earnings calls a key monitoring event.

**⚠️ DATA GAP:** Whether New Devon has publicly commented on its intention to hold, sell, or monetize its Fervo stake is not yet publicly disclosed. This is the most immediately actionable monitoring item following confirmation of the merger close.

### V.B — What the Secondary Market Data Tells Us


Market commentary suggests that Fervo's expected valuation may be lower than what some late-stage investors were led to anticipate during its most recent private funding round. According to reporting by Axios Pro, pre-IPO valuation discussions are said to be in the range of USD 2–3 billion.



Fervo Energy has secondary market activity and it is possible to buy or sell shares. Investor demand is outstripping supply of shares available at this time.


**Synthesis:** The secondary market data presents an apparent paradox — demand outstripping supply yet prices ranging from $8.16 to $10.04 (implying $2.3B–$2.86B valuation depending on share count). The resolution is: (1) supply is suppressed by Fervo's ROFR on transfers (common in late-stage VC); (2) buyers are pricing toward the IPO range ($2–3B) rather than above it; (3) the "demand outstripping supply" statement is a marketing characterization by a secondary market broker, not a price signal. Secondary markets are not confirming a premium; they are confirming that the IPO target range ($2–3B) is consistent with where arms-length buyers are willing to transact.

This is standard project finance M&A mechanics but not obvious from the headline number.

- PIPE at $2.0B pre-money: **below** Series E entry → **anti-dilution trigger** for Series E
- PIPE at $4B pre-money: **strongly above** → **no trigger; existing investors benefit**

|---|---|---|---|---|
| $500M PIPE @ $3B | $3.0B | $3.5B | 14.3% | No anti-dilution trigger |
| $1B PIPE @ $3B | $3.0B | $4.0B | 25.0% | No anti-dilution trigger |
| $500M PIPE @ $4B | $4.0B | $4.5B | 11.1% | No trigger; existing investors benefit |

**The Strategic Logic of a PIPE Before IPO:** A PIPE at $4B pre-money from, say, a major infrastructure fund (BlackRock, KKR, Brookfield) or a strategic (Microsoft, Amazon for data center power, Southern Company for grid integration) would:
(1) Set a credible institutional valuation anchor above Series E
(2) Provide additional construction/development capital
(3) Enable the IPO to be structured at $4B+ with a comparable investor acting as anchor
(4) Resolve the "underwater Series E" problem if the PIPE is above $2.86B post-money

This is the most elegant solution to the IPO valuation problem — and it is notable that no such PIPE has been announced.

### Scenario 3: IPO at $2.5B (Low-End; Potential Down-Round Mechanics)

#### A. The Down-Round Problem


Following its Series E round in late 2025, Fervo Energy reached a post-money valuation of approximately $2.86 billion. Current projections for its 2026 IPO suggest a target valuation range between $2 billion and $3 billion.



This highlights an unresolved question in capital markets: how to price firm, low-carbon energy technologies that combine infrastructure-like characteristics with venture-style technical risk. Geothermal often occupies an uncomfortable middle ground. Once built, geothermal plants behave like stable infrastructure assets. Before that point, they require investors to absorb subsurface risk that is difficult to diversify and slow to de-risk.


**This is the core IPO valuation problem.** At the time of IPO (Summer 2026), Cape Station Ph.I will be either: (a) commercially operating (October 2026 target); or (b) still in late construction (if any slippage occurs). The IPO will price the asset as one or the other — and public markets apply very different multiples to "operating geothermal infrastructure" vs. "pre-revenue construction-stage geothermal."

#### B. Project Financing's Impact on IPO Pricing: The $650M Unlock

If the project financing closes before the S-1 goes public:
- Fervo can present Cape Station as **bankably financed, 100% contracted, construction-stage**
- Investment-grade lenders (RBC, HSBC, SMBC) have validated the credit
- Revenue model (15-year PPAs with Google, SoCal Edison, Shell) is confirmed
- Public market can apply infrastructure-like DCF multiples to contracted cash flows
- **Result: IPO pricing toward $3–4B range becomes defensible**

If the project financing has NOT closed before the IPO:
- Fervo carries $145.6M bridge debt maturing concurrent with IPO
- Project is pre-bankable — public market applies venture discount
- **Result: IPO pricing toward $2–2.5B range**

#### C. QIPO Threshold: The Blocking Mechanism

If the Series E was issued with a QIPO price threshold at or near the Series E implied price per share (standard protective provision for $462M round):

**At $2.5B IPO pricing:** The QIPO condition fails → preferred does NOT auto-convert → Fervo cannot proceed with IPO without:
1. Charter amendment lowering the QIPO threshold → requires majority preferred vote → B Capital must consent (as Series E largest holder)
2. B Capital's consent would be negotiated — they might accept a lower threshold in exchange for: (a) IPO price floor commitment; (b) backstop guarantee; (c) enhanced registration rights; or (d) price adjustment mechanism

**This is B Capital's most important structural lever over the IPO process.** Even without a formal blocking right per se, control of the Series E vote on a charter amendment makes B Capital effectively the gatekeeper of the IPO at sub-$2.86B pricing.

#### D. Lock-Up and Post-IPO Dynamics

**Standard 180-day lock-up applies to all pre-IPO holders.**

| Holder | Stake | Lock-Up Expiry (est.) | Incentive at Lock-Up |
|---|---|---|---|
| New Devon (15%) | ~15% | ~Jan 2027 (if Jul 2026 IPO) | **Strong sell incentive** — geothermal is non-core for shale operator |
| Series E holders | $462M invested | ~Jan 2027 | **Depends on stock price vs. $2.86B** — if below, underwater; forced to hold or sell at loss |
| BEV, DCVC, Capricorn (early rounds) | Large common position | ~Jan 2027 | **Strong gain** at any IPO price; will likely sell |
| CalSTRS | Multi-round | ~Jan 2027 | **Strong sell** — over PE target |
| CPP Investments | Multi-round | ~Jan 2027 | **Likely sell** — institutional liquidity mandate |
| Founders/Employees | Common | ~Jan 2027 (or 10b5-1 plans) | Depends on personal financial planning |

**Lock-up expiry in January 2027 would create a massive secondary supply wave** if the stock trades at or below the IPO price. New Devon alone at 15% would represent billions in potential supply. Early VCs (DCVC, Congruent, BEV) at 10–20× returns would be motivated sellers. CalSTRS at over-PE-target would need to reduce. The combination creates a predictable Day 181 overhang risk that every IPO banker would need to model.

---

## PART IX: NOTABLE ANOMALIES — COMPLETE, PRIORITIZED, AND UPDATED

---

### 🚨 ANOMALY 1 — THE PROJECT FINANCING BINARY IS THE SINGLE MOST CONSEQUENTIAL UNRESOLVED FACT [HIGHEST PRIORITY — UPDATED]


Fervo Energy is set to close project financing for its USD 650M Cape Station project in what would be the first next-generation geothermal project financing. The deal will be the first formal project financing for superhot rock geothermal technology and the company.
 This has not been confirmed as closed as of March 18, 2026.

**Why this is Anomaly #1:** Every other risk in the analysis is secondary to this binary. If it closes: bridge retired, bankable asset, IPO at $3B+, clean consent map. If it doesn't close before the May 2026 public S-1 filing deadline: the S-1 must disclose the bridge maturity risk, road show investors apply a construction discount, IPO prices at $2–2.5B, QIPO threshold may be breached, B Capital's blocking right may be triggered, and the entire timing cascade collapses. The project financing is the keystone of the entire capital structure.

---

### 🚨 ANOMALY 2 — DEVON/COTERRA MERGER HAS CLOSED: NEW DEVON IS NOW A ~15% FERVO HOLDER WITH NO STRATEGIC INTEREST IN GEOTHERMAL [NEW — CRITICAL]


Devon Energy has closed its merger with Coterra Energy, creating a larger U.S. shale producer with a broader asset base. The combined company is targeting substantial pre-tax synergies and higher planned shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks.


New Devon is now a $58B shale operator with $5B in target pro forma free cash flow, focused on Delaware Basin oil and Marcellus gas. Its Fervo stake (~15%, value ~$430M at $2.86B, ~$525M at $3.5B) is a small, illiquid, non-core holding in a pre-revenue geothermal developer. New Devon's management team (Clay Gaspar, Tom Jorden) built their careers in shale, not geothermal. The pre-closing covenants that prevented Devon from acting on its Fervo stake are now lifted.

**The anomaly:** New Devon is simultaneously: (a) Fervo's largest single equity bloc (~15%); (b) the Series D lead with associated governance rights; and (c) the most motivated potential seller in the entire cap table. If New Devon announces its intent to sell its Fervo stake before or concurrent with the IPO, it would: (i) create a massive secondary supply signal that depresses IPO pricing; (ii) potentially trigger ROFR provisions in Fervo's IRA; and (iii) destabilize the Series D governance structure at a critical pre-IPO moment.

---

### 🚨 ANOMALY 3 — CAPE STATION OCTOBER 2026 DELIVERY DATE CREATES A POST-IPO CONSTRUCTION RISK WINDOW [NEW — CRITICAL]


The project's initial 100-megawatt installation is on track to start delivering power to the grid in October 2026, which will make it the first commercial-scale enhanced geothermal project to hit such a milestone worldwide, according to Fervo.


But also: 
Phase I: 100 MW — initial commercial delivery expected in 2026, with first power potentially as early as late 2026 or early 2027 based on ongoing construction.


**The anomaly:** A Summer 2026 IPO pricing in July 2026 means Cape Station Ph.I will be **3 months from commercial operation at IPO date.** This is the most dangerous window for an IPO: close enough to operationality to justify an infrastructure valuation, but far enough away that any construction delay creates post-IPO liability risk. The S-1 will need to disclose October 2026 as the target date. If Cape Station slips to early 2027 (which the cautionary source acknowledges as possible), Fervo would be a public company disclosing a material construction delay — with all the securities law exposure that entails. The lawyers drafting the S-1 risk factors are currently writing some of the most consequential prose in the geothermal industry's history.

---

### ⚠️ ANOMALY 4 — SERIES E INVESTORS ARE UNDERWATER AT THE IPO LOW-END, AND THE GAP IS CONFIRMED ACROSS MULTIPLE SOURCES [CONFIRMED ACROSS THREE RESEARCH ROUNDS]


Fervo Energy's reported confidential IPO filing is reviving an old geothermal question: what, exactly, will public markets pay for drilling risk and scale-up claims? Market commentary suggests that Fervo's expected valuation may be lower than what some late-stage investors were led to anticipate during its most recent private funding round. According to reporting by Axios Pro, pre-IPO valuation discussions are said to be in the range of USD 2–3 billion.


**Magnitude of the problem:** B Capital led a $462M round at $2.86B post-money in December 2025. Three months later, the IPO target low-end is $860M below their entry. This is not a small rounding error — it is a structural mismatch between private round valuations (driven by competition for oversubscribed deals and optimistic geothermal forecasts) and public market pricing discipline (driven by comparable company analysis, revenue multiples, and construction risk discounts). **The resolution of this gap — either through the project financing closing, Cape Station coming online, or an IPO price negotiation with B Capital — is the defining event of Fervo's 2026.**

---

### ⚠️ ANOMALY 5 — THE MERCURIA TRIPLE-ROLE CONFLICT IS STRUCTURALLY UNPRECEDENTED IN CLEAN ENERGY FINANCE [CONFIRMED ACROSS THREE RESEARCH ROUNDS]

Mercuria holds: (1) $100M corporate term loan with change-of-control covenants; (2) Series E equity participation; and (3) the relationship of one of the world's largest commodity energy traders with a geothermal energy developer. 
David Ulrey, CFO of Fervo Energy, stated: "With this capital in place, Fervo's ability to de-risk and finance multi-GW EGS project areas is becoming a reality. These investments demonstrate what we've known all along: Fervo's combination of technical excellence, commercial readiness, and market opportunity makes us a natural partner for serious energy capital."


In any M&A, Mercuria simultaneously: (a) must consent as lender (or be prepaid); (b) votes as equity holder; and (c) may have a commercial interest in who acquires Fervo's geothermal output. No governance protocol has been publicly disclosed for how Mercuria will manage this conflict. An acquirer negotiating with Mercuria for COC waiver while Mercuria also votes on the stockholder consent is a structurally unusual situation that any M&A committee must specifically address.

---

### ⚠️ ANOMALY 6 — CENTAURUS/ARNOLD DUAL POSITION HAS BEEN STRUCTURALLY ALTERED BY THE PROJECT FINANCING [UPDATED]


This investment builds on Centaurus Capital's recent $75 million preferred equity commitment to support construction of Cape Station Phase I, with Centaurus also participating in the Series E round.


The insertion of $650M of senior bank debt above Centaurus's $75M project preferred means Centaurus's "first-loss protection" is now: banks must lose $650M before Centaurus loses a dollar at the project level. This converts what was described as a structural protection into a mezzanine position in a highly leveraged project. The dual position remains advantageous in going-concern scenarios (all three transaction scenarios) but the downside protection is substantially weakened in any project stress scenario.

Additionally: John Arnold's board membership at Breakthrough Energy (which separately holds $100M project preferred alongside Centaurus at the project level) creates overlapping governance influence over two independent preferred equity instruments at the same asset. No intercreditor agreement between BEC and Centaurus has been publicly disclosed.

---

### ⚠️ ANOMALY 7 — GOOGLE'S STRUCTURAL CONFLICT INTENSIFIES AS CAPE STATION APPROACHES COMPLETION [CONFIRMED ACROSS THREE RESEARCH ROUNDS]


This 3.5-MW facility in Nevada was built with support from Google, which is also working with Fervo to develop 115 MW of geothermal energy to power the tech giant's data centers in the state. Now, along with the IPO filing, Fervo is gearing up to mark an even bigger achievement: completing the first 100 MW of its 500-MW Cape Station project in Utah and delivering power to the grid in October.


Google holds: (a) equity (Series E) with information rights; (b) the anchor offtake PPA for Cape Station; (c) a co-development agreement for 115MW in Nevada; and (d) the institutional knowledge from Project Red (the 2021–2023 pilot). As Cape Station approaches commercial operation and Fervo's IPO looms, the value of Fervo's technology platform increases precisely as Google's information advantage (as an informed insider) also increases. Any M&A process where Google is a potential acquirer — and Google's data center power needs make it a plausible strategic acquirer — would require extensive conflict management protocols not currently visible in any public disclosure.

---

### ⚠️ ANOMALY 8 — SECONDARY MARKET PRICING SHOWS A SPREAD ($8.16 vs. $10.04) INDICATING UNRELIABLE PRICE DISCOVERY [NEW]

Two secondary market price estimates for Fervo stock are now available: 
Fervo Energy stock price is built algorithmically using secondary market and reference data
 at $8.16/share (Notice.co), while Hiive showed $10.04/share (March 5, 2026). The $1.88/share spread represents approximately a 23% difference — massive for a company purportedly trading in an active secondary market. Combined with Hiive's disclosure of only 5 live orders, this confirms that secondary market pricing for Fervo is **thin, unreliable, and not a valid substitute for actual price discovery**. 
Investor demand is outstripping supply of shares available at this time
 — but "demand outstripping supply" at a thin market with ROFR-suppressed supply is not the same as a healthy, liquid market validating a $2.86B+ valuation.

---

### ⚠️ ANOMALY 9 — THE IPO IS A RACE AMONG FIVE SIMULTANEOUS CLOCKS [SYNTHESIS — FINAL]

Fervo's Summer 2026 IPO requires all five of the following to occur in sequence before the roadshow begins:

| Clock | Required Outcome | Deadline | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. $650M Project Financing | Must close | March–April 2026 | ⚠️ Not yet confirmed closed |
| 2. XRA Bridge Retirement | Follows from #1 | Concurrent with PF close | ⚠️ Conditional on #1 |
| 3. Devon/Coterra Merger | Already closed | Q2 2026 | ✅ Substantially confirmed closed |
| 4. New Devon Fervo Stake | Must signal hold (not sell) before IPO | Before public S-1 filing | ❌ No public statement |
| 5. Cape Station Ph.I on Track | Must confirm October 2026 delivery | Before public S-1 | ⚠️ On track but slippage risk |
| 6. Public S-1 Filing | Triggers 15-day roadshow clock | ~May 2026 | ❌ Not yet filed |

**Only one of these six items is confirmed resolved (Devon merger closed). The other five are pending.** This is an extraordinary concentration of unresolved binary risks for a company 8–12 weeks from a target public offering. The IPO's feasibility is not in question (Fervo has strong assets, technology, and investor support), but the **pricing and timing** remain deeply uncertain pending resolution of these five clocks.

---
